title: Von Neumann Computer Architecture
examTip: Von Neumann architecture has 5 components: CPU (CU + ALU), Memory Unit, Input Unit, Output Unit, connected by a single shared Bus. KEY concept: stored program — instructions and data share the same memory and bus (von Neumann bottleneck). Harvard architecture separates program and data memory.

blocks.label: Memory Unit
sublabels: Addressable cells: each has unique address

buses.label: System Bus: Address Bus + Data Bus + Control Bus  (Von Neumann Bottleneck: shared by instructions and data)

theory:
The Von Neumann Architecture, proposed by mathematician John von Neumann in 1945 based on the EDVAC computer design, is the foundational design used by virtually all modern general-purpose computers. Its defining characteristic is the stored-program concept — both program instructions and data are stored together in the same memory, connected to the CPU via a single shared bus.

Five Core Components:

1. CPU (Central Processing Unit): Consists of the Control Unit (CU) — which fetches instructions from memory (using MAR and MDR registers), decodes them, and generates control signals to coordinate all components — and the ALU (Arithmetic & Logic Unit) which executes arithmetic and logical operations. Registers include PC (Program Counter pointing to next instruction), IR (Instruction Register holding current opcode), MAR (Memory Address Register), MDR (Memory Data Register), and the Accumulator.

2. Memory Unit: A single unified memory that stores both program instructions and data values. Memory is organized as addressable cells (bytes or words). This shared memory is accessed via the Address Bus (specifies location) and Data Bus (carries data).

3. Input Unit: Devices that transfer data into the computer (keyboard, mouse, scanner, network interface).

4. Output Unit: Devices that present processed results to the user (monitor, printer, speakers).

5. Buses: Three buses connect all components — Address Bus (unidirectional, CPU→Memory), Data Bus (bidirectional), Control Bus (read/write/interrupt signals).

Von Neumann Bottleneck: Since instructions and data share the same bus and memory, the CPU cannot simultaneously fetch an instruction and read/write data — limiting performance. Harvard Architecture solves this by using separate memories and buses for instructions and data (used in 8051 microcontrollers and modern cache designs).
keyPoints: Stored-program concept: instructions and data stored together in same memory — shared bus
5 components: Input → CPU (CU + ALU + Registers) + Memory → Output, connected by system bus
Von Neumann Bottleneck: shared bus for data and instructions limits CPU performance
Harvard Architecture difference: separate memories/buses for program code and data (8051, DSPs)
Instruction cycle: Fetch (PC→MAR→MDR→IR) → Decode (CU interprets IR) → Execute (ALU operates)
useCases: All general-purpose computers: desktops, laptops, servers — all use Von Neumann architecture
Smartphones and tablets — ARM processors follow Von Neumann with Harvard-style cache hierarchy
Historical foundation: ENIAC, EDVAC, EDSAC — all early computers based on this design
